_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Greene County, PA. Formed 1796 from Washington County. County seat at Waynesburg. The most rural and isolated county in the cluster, bordering Virginia/West Virginia. Record survival is good from county formation, though the county's small population means thinner records than its neighbors.

current_courthouse.location: Waynesburg, PA — 93 E. High Street (one of the oldest courthouses in continuous use in western PA)

previous_courthouses.fate: Log courthouse. Greene County was carved from Washington County and was sparsely populated. The first courthouse reflected the frontier character of the county.
Brick courthouse. Served the growing agricultural county. Replaced by the current Greek Revival building in 1850.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or catastrophic loss
record_losses.description: Greene County's courthouse — the 1850 Greek Revival building, still in use — has not experienced a catastrophic fire. Records run from the county's 1796 formation. Pre-1796 records for the Greene County area are filed under Washington County. Greene County's records are thinner than those of its more populous neighbors because the county's population remained small and agricultural, but what exists is intact.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in Greene County after 1796, the records survive. The thinness of the records reflects the county's small population, not record loss. Greene County families who left few traces did so because subsistence farming on the Virginia border generated little paperwork, not because a courthouse burned.
record_losses.source: Pennsylvania Historical and Museum Commission. Guide to the Record Groups in the Pennsylvania State Archives.

deeds.completeness: Good — continuous from county formation. Pre-1796 deeds for the Greene area filed under Washington County.
deeds.gaps: No known significant gaps. The county's early deed books reflect a small, rural population — fewer transactions per year than Allegheny or Westmoreland.
deeds.repository: Greene County Recorder of Deeds, Waynesburg; Pennsylvania State Archives microfilm

wills_estates.gaps: Small population means fewer wills per decade. Intestate estates settled through Orphans' Court.
wills_estates.repository: Greene County Register of Wills; Pennsylvania State Archives microfilm

marriages.completeness: Marriage licenses from 1885. Pre-1885 marriages through church records and newspaper notices.
marriages.gaps: The 89-year gap between formation (1796) and marriage license law (1885) is filled by Baptist, Methodist, and Presbyterian church records. Greene County's churches were the primary record-keepers for vital events in this period.
marriages.repository: Greene County Clerk of the Orphans' Court; Pennsylvania State Archives

court_minutes.repository: Greene County Prothonotary; Pennsylvania State Archives microfilm

vital_records.completeness: Spotty. Greene County's rural isolation and small population meant compliance was slower than urban areas.
vital_records.repository: Pennsylvania Department of Health; Pennsylvania State Archives

substitute_sources.description: Greene County borders West Virginia (formerly Virginia). Families routinely crossed the state line for church, trade, and marriage. Virginia county records for Monongalia County (WV) and Ohio County (WV) may contain records for Greene County families.
substitute_sources.repository: West Virginia Division of Culture and History; Monongalia County Clerk; Ohio County Clerk
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for border families. A Greene County family that attended church in Virginia or sold livestock at the Wheeling market may appear in Virginia/WV records but not in Greene County's.
substitute_sources.source: Sims, Edgar B. Making a State: Formation of West Virginia. Charleston, WV, 1956.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1800–1940. Greene County first enumerated in 1800. Agricultural schedules (1850–1880) are particularly valuable for this farming county.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential baseline. Agricultural schedules document farm size, livestock, and crop production and distinguish prosperous farmers from subsistence operators.

substitute_sources.description: Greene County's coal industry developed later than the Connellsville region — primarily early twentieth century. Company records from coal operations in central and eastern Greene County document miners and their families.
substitute_sources.repository: Pennsylvania State Archives; Waynesburg University Special Collections; Greene County Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Payroll and housing records document immigrant and native-born miners. Greene County's coal was deep-mined bituminous, and the company towns that served the mines generated records similar to those in Fayette and Westmoreland counties.

narrative_hooks: Greene County was the most rural and isolated county in western Pennsylvania — if {ancestor_name} lived there after 1796, the records survive in one of the oldest courthouses in the region, but the thinness of the paper trail reflects the quiet life of a border farming county, not record loss.

Greene County sits on the Virginia border, and {ancestor_name}'s family may have crossed that line routinely — church in Virginia, trade at Wheeling, kin in Monongalia County. The state line was a line on a map, not a wall.

Pennsylvania marriage licenses started in 1885 — for {ancestor_name} in Greene County before that date, the Baptist or Methodist church records are the likeliest evidence, because the county's churches were the record-keepers for a population too scattered for civil registration.

Pre-1796 records for {ancestor_name}'s land in the Greene County area are filed under Washington County — the parent county whose records the researcher must check for the earliest deeds and court appearances.
source_references: Pennsylvania Historical and Museum Commission. Guide to the Record Groups in the Pennsylvania State Archives.
Sims, Edgar B. Making a State: Formation of West Virginia. Charleston, WV, 1956.
Hanna, William S. History of Greene County, Pennsylvania. Self-published, 1882.
